📘 Chapter 4 Quiz — Input and Output with Streams
PART 1 — Multiple Choice

1️⃣ What is a stream in C++?
B. A flow of data between a program and an input/output device

2️⃣ Which object is used for standard output?
B. cout

3️⃣ What header file is required for formatting manipulators like setw() and setprecision()?
A. <iomanip>

4️⃣ What does this do?

cout << boolalpha << true;
B. Prints true

5️⃣ What does setw(5) do?
B. Sets the output field width to 5 characters

----------

PART 2 — True or False

6️⃣ cin >> stops reading input at whitespace.

(true)

7️⃣ getline() can read spaces in a sentence.

(true)

8️⃣ cout.put('A') outputs the character A.

(True)

9️⃣ setprecision(2) affects floating-point output formatting.

(True)

🔟 cin.get(ch) ignores whitespace characters.

(True)

---------

PART 3 — Output Prediction

1️⃣ What will this print?

cout << setw(5) << 10;

= -----10-----(the - is a blank space in this logic)

2️⃣ What will this print?

bool ok = true;
cout << ok << endl;
cout << boolalpha << ok;

= true (since boolalpha was used, not noboolalpha)

3️⃣ What will this print?

cout << setprecision(2) << 3.14159;

-----------

PART 4 — Fix the Error

1️⃣ What is wrong with this code?

cin << name;

Correct it.

= cin >> name

2️⃣ Fix this program

#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main()
{
string name;
cout << "Enter name:";
getline(name);
cout << name;
}


= the line getline(name); should be: getline(cin, name)
-----------
Bonus (Thinking Question)

Why is getline(cin, text) often preferred over:

cin >> text;

when reading user input?
= for more prpoer string getter syntax and getline will read all the users input until pressed ENTER or <return>
